summary: The SE Beltline is buzzing with new development as Reynoldstown finally sees progress on a long-stalled housing project. Meanwhile, political tensions are running high as both DeKalb and Atlanta grapple with ICE enforcement issues.

topStory.headline: Reynoldstown Corner Near Beltline Finally Breaks Ground
topStory.summary: After sitting empty for nearly five years, that cleared lot at Chester Avenue and Fulton Terrace is finally seeing action. Embry Development is moving forward with 85 residential units just a block from the Eastside Trail. It's another sign that the Beltline corridor keeps drawing serious investment, even when projects hit delays.
topStory.body:
You know that vacant corner in Reynoldstown where Chester meets Fulton Terrace? The one that's been sitting there like a missing tooth since they cleared the buildings back in 2021? Well, grab your hard hats because construction is finally underway.

Embry Development Company took over this project from Toll Brothers two years ago, inheriting permits for 85 residential units about a block west of the Beltline's Eastside Trail. The 2-acre site is getting a mix of townhomes and for-sale condos, which frankly is exactly what this stretch of Memorial Drive needs. The area has been crying out for more housing options that actually fit the neighborhood's scale.

This kind of stop-and-start development story is becoming all too familiar along the Beltline. Projects get announced with fanfare, hit funding snags or market shifts, then eventually find their footing with new developers. At least this one's moving again. When you're walking or biking the trail through Reynoldstown, you'll have some actual housing to look at instead of that sad empty lot.
topStory.imageAlt: Construction equipment at the Reynoldstown development site near the Beltline

stories.imageAlt: Construction equipment at the Reynoldstown development site near the Beltline

stories.headline: Decatur's 'Missing Middle' Village Starts Marketing Next Week
stories.summary: Cottages at Midway is about to launch sales for 18 standalone houses built around shared courtyards near downtown Decatur. With median home values hitting $700k in the area, this mixed-income project is trying to bring some sanity back to ITP pricing. The soft launch starts next week for the community on Midway Road.
stories.body:
Finally, someone's doing something smart about Atlanta's missing middle housing crisis. Cottages at Midway is getting ready to start drumming up buyers for 18 new houses on a couple of underused lots on Midway Road in unincorporated Decatur.

The project is designed as a village-style community with houses arranged around shared courtyards and greenspace. It's exactly the kind of thoughtful development we need more of—especially when you consider that median home values in downtown Decatur have climbed north of $700,000. These aren't going to be cheap, but they're aiming for more accessible price points than what's currently available.

This is the kind of infill development that makes sense. Instead of another mega-development eating up farmland in Gwinnett, we're getting thoughtful density on a 2.1-acre site that's been sitting there doing nothing. The soft launch starts next week, so if you've been priced out of Decatur proper but still want to be close to the action, keep your eyes peeled.
stories.imageAlt: Site map showing the layout of Cottages at Midway development

stories.headline: West End's Rare For-Sale Townhomes Start Taking Shape
stories.summary: GW Parkside (formerly Hopkins and White Townhomes) is finally rising along the Beltline in the West End. Ackerman & Co. is building 23 new units with the first seven hitting the market this spring. It's pretty rare to find new for-sale townhomes along this stretch of the trail, so this could fill a real gap.
stories.body:
Here's something you don't see every day: new for-sale townhomes along the Beltline in Southwest Atlanta. GW Parkside on Hopkins Street is starting to take shape after Ackerman & Co. started demolition about a year ago.

The developer, who also owns Lee + White and all its cool food and retail offerings, is building 23 units with the first seven expected to hit the market this spring. This is the same team that knows how to do thoughtful development in these neighborhoods, so expectations are high.

What makes this project interesting is location and rarity. Finding new for-sale townhomes along this stretch of the Beltline is like finding a parking spot at Ponce City Market on a Saturday. The West End has been seeing steady investment and improvement, but most of the new housing has been rental apartments. These townhomes could give people a chance to actually own something within walking distance of the trail.
stories.imageAlt: Rendering of GW Parkside townhomes under construction in West End

stories.headline: New Asian-American Spot Mushi Ni Opens in Inman Park
stories.summary: Chefs Michael Le and Tanya Jimenez just opened their first restaurant on Elizabeth Street, right off the Freedom Park Trail. Expect soft shell crab bao, Peking chicken platters, and Thai tea martinis. The pop-up-turned-restaurant is serving lunch and dinner with serious cocktail game.
stories.body:
Inman Park just got a little more delicious. Mushi Ni opened yesterday on Elizabeth Street, and it's exactly the kind of neighborhood spot this area needed. Chef-owners Michael Le and Tanya Jimenez have serious restaurant pedigree (she worked for Gordon Ramsay in New York), and they're bringing that expertise to Asian-American comfort food.

The menu hits all the right notes: soft shell crab bao, chicken longanisa bao, umami fries, and those Instagram-worthy crab Rangoon puffs. The Peking chicken platter feeds two people for $25, which feels almost generous these days. But what caught my attention are the cocktails—tamarind whiskey sours and Thai tea martinis suggest they're taking the drink program as seriously as the food.

The location is perfect too, just off the Freedom Park Trail where you can easily walk or bike over from the Beltline connection. After all those pop-up events around town, it's great to see them settle into a permanent space where the neighborhood can really get to know their cooking.
stories.imageAlt: Peking chicken platter at Mushi Ni restaurant

stories.headline: Robert Montwaid's Eastside Food Hall Opens Mid-April
stories.summary: The creator of New York's Gansevoort Market is opening Vela Park Food Works near East Lake Golf Club in mid-April. Six chef-driven concepts, craft cocktails, and roomy outdoor dining are coming to the nearly 12-acre rental community. It's Montwaid's third Atlanta food destination.
stories.body:
Get ready for another serious food hall, but this time it's headed to the eastside. Robert Montwaid, the mastermind behind New York's legendary Gansevoart Market, is opening Vela Park Food Works in mid-April as part of the new Vela Park community near East Lake Golf Club.

This isn't just another grab-and-go food court situation. Montwaid is bringing six chef-driven concepts along with a full craft cocktail program and plenty of outdoor dining space. Given his track record in New York and his previous Atlanta projects, expectations are running high for the quality and creativity of the food offerings.

What makes this particularly interesting is the location. East Lake has been quietly developing into a more dynamic neighborhood, and having a destination food hall could really accelerate that transformation. The 12-acre rental community it's part of suggests this is designed to serve both residents and draw people from across the eastside looking for something new.
stories.imageAlt: Interior rendering of Vela Park Food Works food hall

stories.headline: Texas-Style Lewis Barbecue Lands on the Beltline
stories.summary: Pitmaster John Lewis just opened his smokehouse at Ansley Mall behind The Cook's Warehouse, complete with panoramic windows overlooking the new pedestrian bridge to the Beltline. Six 22-foot smokers run 24/7 in what might be Georgia's fanciest engine room, turning out some of the city's best brisket.
stories.body:
The Beltline just got a serious barbecue upgrade. Lewis Barbecue opened at Ansley Mall with a setup unlike anything I've seen in Atlanta. Pitmaster John Lewis put his six 22-foot smokers upstairs in a loft with panoramic windows overlooking the new pedestrian bridge to the Beltline. It looks like the fanciest engine room in Georgia.

These aren't your typical backyard smokers either. Lewis helped weld them out of 1,000-gallon propane tanks, and they run 24 hours a day in staggered eight-hour shifts. That's the kind of serious operation that produces consistently excellent brisket. The Austin and Charleston locations have built quite a reputation, so Atlanta expectations are high.

What I love about this location is the Beltline connection. You can literally walk or bike over from the trail, grab some of the best barbecue in the city, and head back to the park. Plus, having those massive smokers visible through the windows adds some serious theater to the whole experience. It's barbecue as performance art.
stories.imageAlt: Barbecue platter at Lewis Barbecue with smoke and heat elements

stories.headline: Atlanta Council Advances ICE Detention Limits
stories.summary: The City Council's Public Safety Committee unanimously moved forward two resolutions: one barring ICE detention facilities within city limits, and another requiring Atlanta police to document when ICE agents are present during their operations. The full council votes April 20, while firefighters are still mad about their contract dispute.
stories.body:
Atlanta's taking a clear stance on immigration enforcement. The Public Safety Committee unanimously advanced two resolutions that would significantly limit ICE operations within the city. One bars any new ICE detention facilities from being built in Atlanta, while the other requires APD to document whenever ICE agents are present during police operations.

The timing is particularly pointed given that ICE agents are currently stationed at Hartsfield-Jackson because of the partial government shutdown. Dozens of residents showed up to advocate for these measures, making it clear this isn't just symbolic politics but something people genuinely care about.

But the committee meeting wasn't just about ICE. Firefighters and their supporters also packed the room to criticize Mayor Dickens for refusing to sign the city's first-ever firefighter union contract. The council already ratified it, but Dickens won't put his signature on it. That's creating some serious political tension as we head into what's likely to be a contentious budget season.
stories.imageAlt: Atlanta City Council Public Safety Committee meeting with residents in attendance

stories.headline: DeKalb Parents Protest School Closure Plans at Lakeside
stories.summary: Hundreds of frustrated parents showed up at Lakeside High for DeKalb's latest school closure input session. Oak Grove and Evansdale families took to the streets beforehand, demanding a student-first approach to the controversial Student Assignment Project. Parents are calling the whole process a farce.
stories.body:
The DeKalb school closure drama reached a boiling point Monday night at Lakeside High. Hundreds of angry parents packed the auditorium for another "input session" on the district's latest proposal for closing schools, but many had already given up on the process being anything more than theater.

Before the meeting even started, families from Oak Grove and Evansdale elementary schools were outside protesting with signs calling for small schools and a people-first approach. Samuel Howe, an Oak Grove parent, didn't mince words: "This is not a process. This is a farce, and they're not listening."

What's particularly frustrating for parents is that this feels like déjà vu. The district has been through multiple rounds of closure proposals, community input sessions, and revised plans. But families still feel like the fundamental decisions have already been made and they're just going through the motions of community engagement. With the district facing serious budget pressures, something's got to give, but parents want actual transparency about the decision-making process.
stories.imageAlt: Parents and children protesting outside DeKalb County school meeting

stories.headline: Decatur's West Howard Complete Streets Nearly Finished
stories.summary: After nearly a decade in the works, Decatur's push to calm traffic on West Howard Avenue is almost done. The mile-long project between downtown Decatur and Atlanta includes new sidewalks, a two-way cycle track with concrete barriers, and infrastructure that should make the corridor much more people-friendly.
stories.body:
Remember when West Howard Avenue felt like a drag strip between Decatur and Atlanta? Well, those days are numbered. The Complete Streets project that's been in the works for nearly a decade is finally wrapping up, and the transformation is pretty dramatic.

The roughly one-mile stretch from Commerce Drive near Decatur High School to Paden Circle by East Lake MARTA station now has proper sidewalks, repaving, and a two-way cycle track with concrete barriers for most of the route. It's exactly the kind of infrastructure that makes neighborhoods more walkable and bikeable instead of just car-friendly.

What's particularly smart about this project is how it connects to the broader transit network. The western end hooks up with East Lake MARTA, while the eastern portion flows right into downtown Decatur. For people trying to get around without a car, this creates a much safer and more comfortable route between two major destinations.
stories.imageAlt: New concrete-protected bike lanes on West Howard Avenue in Decatur

stories.headline: ICE Agents Deploy to Atlanta Airport During Shutdown
stories.summary: Armed federal immigration officers in tactical gear are patrolling Hartsfield-Jackson as the partial government shutdown drives massive TSA staffing shortages. The agents aren't doing security screenings or immigration enforcement, just providing security while travelers face four-hour wait times to get through checkpoints.
stories.body:
The partial government shutdown has gotten so bad that ICE agents are now walking the terminals at Hartsfield-Jackson Atlanta International Airport. The armed officers in tactical gear aren't doing security screenings or immigration enforcement, they're just there to provide extra security while TSA agents work without pay.

The situation at the airport is pretty grim. Travelers are being told to prepare for at least four-hour wait times to get through security, and that's on a good day. Nearly 3,500 TSA officers nationwide called out of work Monday, with Atlanta seeing particularly high absence rates. When 41% of TSA agents don't show up for work, you get the kind of chaos we're seeing.

Mayor Dickens put out a statement Sunday assuring residents that the ICE agents won't be doing immigration enforcement during this deployment. But seeing federal immigration officers patrolling the world's busiest airport because of a budget fight in Washington is pretty surreal, even for 2026 standards.
stories.imageAlt: Federal immigration officers at Hartsfield-Jackson Atlanta International Airport

stories.headline: Man Killed Outside Vine City Market Tuesday Night
stories.summary: Atlanta police are investigating a deadly shooting at a Vine City food mart on Joseph E. Boone Boulevard. The victim was rushed to the hospital but didn't survive. It happened around 9 p.m. near Joseph E. Lowery Boulevard, and police haven't released details about suspects or what led to the gunfire.
stories.body:
Another night, another shooting in Atlanta. This time it was outside a food mart in Vine City on Joseph E. Boone Boulevard, just east of Joseph E. Lowery Boulevard. The shooting happened just before 9 p.m. Tuesday, and the man who was shot died at the hospital despite efforts to save him.

Vine City has been dealing with ongoing public safety challenges, and this incident adds to concerns about violence in the neighborhood. Atlanta police haven't released the victim's identity or any information about suspects, so it's too early to know if this was targeted or random.

What's particularly concerning is that this was one of at least two shootings in the area Tuesday night. About a mile away on Martin Luther King Jr. Drive, there was another shooting outside a barber shop, though police haven't said whether the incidents are connected. For residents trying to go about their daily business, this kind of violence is exhausting and frightening.
stories.imageAlt: Police investigation scene at Vine City food mart

stories.headline: 8-Foot Python Spotted on Atlanta Beltline
stories.summary: Someone captured video of a man handling an 8- to 10-foot snake found along the Beltline, but wildlife officials are warning people not to handle unknown animals. The "Atlanta-Conda" incident has reptile experts concerned about both the snake's welfare and public safety.
stories.body:
Just when you think you've seen everything on the Beltline, someone spots an 8-foot python. Joseph Ragland was walking the trail when he came across what he describes as definitely not a typical Georgia snake, and then watched as "somebody's uncle" came out of nowhere and started yanking it out of the bushes.

The video shows about 10 feet of snake laying on the Beltline, and nobody knows if the man handling it was the owner or just someone who thought they were being helpful. Jason Clark from Southeastern Reptile Rescue is pretty concerned about the whole situation, noting that handling unknown reptiles is dangerous for both the snake and the person.

This is exactly the kind of weird Atlanta story that makes you love this city and worry about it at the same time. The Beltline has become such a central part of life here that of course someone would abandon (or lose) their python on it. Environmental officials are reminding everyone to call professionals when they encounter wildlife they can't identify, rather than trying to wrangle it themselves.
stories.imageAlt: Large python being handled by man on Atlanta Beltline
